1: theme Oh give thanks to the LORD, for he is good, for his steadfast love endures forever! The everlasting steadfast love — the theme of John 3:16 and Romans 8:38-39. The church's Eucharist (Greek: thanksgiving) is the fulfillment of this "give thanks" — Christ institutes the meal of perpetual thanksgiving at the Last Supper, directing the church to remember and proclaim his love until he comes (1 Corinthians 11:26).
2: shadow Let the redeemed of the LORD say so, whom he has redeemed from trouble The redeemed testifying to redemption — the vocation of the church. Revelation 5:9: the four living creatures and elders sing to the Lamb, for you were slain, and by your blood you ransomed people for God from every tribe and language and people and nation. The redeemed of the LORD say so by singing the Lamb's praise in every tongue.
3: shadow and gathered in from the lands, from the east and from the west, from the north and from the south. Gathering from all directions — the eschatological ingathering Christ announces: people will come from east and west, and from north and south, and recline at table in the kingdom of God (Luke 13:29). Matthew 24:31: he will send out his angels... and they will gather his elect from the four winds, from one end of heaven to the other.
4: type Some wandered in desert wastes, finding no way to a city to dwell in; Wandering without a city — the condition of fallen humanity: strangers and exiles on the earth seeking a city (Hebrews 11:13-14). Christ brings the wanderers to the heavenly city: for he has prepared for them a city (Hebrews 11:16). The New Jerusalem descends from heaven (Revelation 21:2) — the city the desert-wanderers have been seeking.
5: shadow hungry and thirsty, their soul fainted within them. Hunger and thirst unto fainting — the destitution Christ addresses: Blessed are those who hunger and thirst for righteousness, for they shall be satisfied (Matthew 5:6). The hunger of v5 is the condition Christ pronounces blessed because he is the bread and water that satisfies: whoever comes to me shall not hunger, and whoever believes in me shall never thirst (John 6:35).
6: type Then they cried to the LORD in their trouble, and he delivered them from their distress. Crying and receiving deliverance — the pattern fulfilled in Christ's cry from the cross (Matthew 27:46) and answered by resurrection (Hebrews 5:7: he offered up prayers and supplications, with loud cries and tears, to him who was able to save him from death, and he was heard because of his reverence). Christ cries out and is delivered; those who cry in his name receive the same answer.
7: shadow He led them by a straight way till they reached a city to dwell in. The straight way to the city — Christ: I am the way, and the truth, and the life; no one comes to the Father except through me (John 14:6). The straight way to the heavenly city is not a road but a person; he both leads and is the path. The wanderers of v4 find the city only through the one who is the way.
8: theme Let them thank the LORD for his steadfast love, for his wondrous works to the children of man! The refrain (repeated in v15, v21, v31) — the response of wonder and thanks to God's redemptive acts. The supreme wondrous work is the resurrection: This is the Lord's doing; it is marvelous in our eyes (Psalms 118:23, quoted at Matthew 21:42). The four-fold refrain of Ps 107 finds its ultimate object in the once-for-all work of Christ.
9: direct for he satisfies the longing soul, and the hungry soul he fills with good things. Luke 1:53 quotes this in the Magnificat: he has filled the hungry with good things. Mary sings the fulfillment of Psalms 107:9 in the Incarnation — God satisfying the longing soul by giving his Son. Christ: I am the bread of life; whoever comes to me shall not hunger, and whoever believes in me shall never thirst (John 6:35).
10: shadow Some sat in darkness and in the shadow of death, prisoners in affliction and in irons, Darkness and shadow of death — Isaiah 9:2 (quoted in Matthew 4:16): the people dwelling in darkness have seen a great light, and for those dwelling in the region and shadow of death, on them a light has dawned. Christ is the light entering the darkness of v10. The prisoners in irons are those to whom he proclaims liberty (Luke 4:18; Isaiah 61:1).
11: shadow for they had rebelled against the words of God, and spurned the counsel of the Most High. Rebellion against God's word as the root of bondage — the human condition Christ addresses at its source. He does not merely release prisoners; he deals with why they are prisoners. He himself is the Word made flesh (John 1:14) — the one they rejected — and his cross deals with the guilt of that rejection at its root.
12: shadow So he bowed their heart down with hard labor; they fell down, with none to help. No helper when the heart bows down — the Good Samaritan parable enacted: Christ sees the one fallen with no helper and comes to help (Luke 10:33-35). He came when there was no one to help (Psalms 22:11): while we were still weak, at the right time Christ died for the ungodly (Romans 5:6).
13: type Then they cried to the LORD in their trouble, and he delivered them from their distress. The second rescue refrain — the pattern of cry-and-deliverance Christ permanently fulfills. Hebrews 5:7: Christ offered up prayers with loud cries and tears and was heard. His rescue from the tomb is the archetypal deliverance from ultimate distress, the pattern for all who cry to him in their trouble.
14: shadow He brought them out of darkness and the shadow of death, and burst their bonds apart. Bonds bursting and coming out of darkness — the resurrection. Acts 2:24: God raised him up, loosing the pangs of death, because it was not possible for him to be held by it. The bonds of death burst at the resurrection; those in Christ are brought out of the shadow of death into the light of everlasting life.
15: theme Let them thank the LORD for his steadfast love, for his wondrous works to the children of man! The second refrain — thanksgiving for steadfast love and wondrous works. The church's perpetual offering: through him then let us continually offer up a sacrifice of praise to God, that is, the fruit of lips that acknowledge his name (Hebrews 13:15). The refrain of Ps 107 is the heartbeat of Christian worship.
16: shadow For he shatters the doors of bronze and cuts in two the bars of iron. Shattering bronze doors and iron bars — Christ's victory over death. The gates of hell shall not prevail against it (Matthew 16:18). Christ descends into death and shatters its doors from the inside (Acts 2:24). The bars of iron imprisoning humanity are cut apart by his resurrection power — I have the keys of Death and Hades (Revelation 1:18).
17: shadow Some were fools through their sinful ways, and because of their iniquities suffered affliction; Sickness as consequence of sin — the connection Christ complicates and ultimately resolves. While not all sickness is direct punishment (John 9:3), the root of all human suffering is the fall. Christ addresses both: he heals the sick and forgives sins (Matthew 9:2-7), dealing with symptoms and cause together.
18: shadow they loathed any kind of food, and they drew near to the gates of death. Drawing near to the gates of death — the terminal condition Christ reverses. He stands outside the tomb of Lazarus and proclaims: I am the resurrection and the life. Whoever believes in me, though he die, yet shall he live (John 11:25). Those at the gates of death find in Christ the one who holds the keys of death and Hades (Revelation 1:18).
19: type Then they cried to the LORD in their trouble, and he delivered them from their distress. The third rescue refrain — cry answered by deliverance. The pattern encoded in the Lord's Prayer: deliver us from evil (Matthew 6:13). Christ teaches his disciples to cry to the Father in distress because he has demonstrated that such cries are heard — his own cry received the ultimate answer in resurrection.
20: direct He sent out his word and healed them, and delivered them from their destruction. God sending his word to heal — the Incarnation as the supreme sending of the Word. John 1:14: the Word became flesh and dwelt among us. The healing word of v20 is no longer just spoken but embodied. Christ heals with a word across distance (Matthew 8:8: only say the word, and my servant will be healed) and into death itself.
21: theme Let them thank the LORD for his steadfast love, for his wondrous works to the children of man! The third refrain — thanksgiving arising from healing and deliverance. The ten lepers are healed; one returns to thank Christ. Was no one found to return and give praise to God except this foreigner? (Luke 17:18). The Samaritan's return is the shape of the refrain enacted — the redeemed soul giving thanks for God's wondrous works.
22: shadow and let them offer sacrifices of thanksgiving, and tell of his deeds in songs of joy! Sacrifices of thanksgiving and songs of joy — the Christian worship pattern. Hebrews 13:15: through him then let us continually offer up a sacrifice of praise to God. The physical sacrifices of the OT are replaced by the sacrifice of praise through Christ, and the songs of joy find their content in the deeds of the one who saved us.
23: shadow Some went down to the sea in ships, doing business on the great waters; Those doing business on the great waters — the disciples Christ calls from their nets (Matthew 4:18-22). He comes to those at work on the sea, calls them to follow him, and makes them fishers of men. The sea remains in view throughout his ministry — where he walks (Matthew 14:25), stills storms (Matthew 8:26), and provides miraculous catches (John 21:6).
24: shadow they saw the deeds of the LORD, his wondrous works in the deep. Wondrous works in the deep — the disciples witnessing Christ's sea miracles. When Jesus walks on water, those in the boat worship him: Truly you are the Son of God (Matthew 14:33). When he calms the storm: What sort of man is this, that even winds and sea obey him? (Matthew 8:27). The wondrous works of the LORD in the deep are the signature acts of the incarnate Son.
25: shadow For he commanded and raised the stormy wind, which lifted up the waves of the sea. Commanding the storm — authority over wind and wave that belongs to Christ. Mark 4:39: he rebuked the wind and said to the sea, Peace! Be still! And the wind ceased, and there was a great calm. The disciples' reaction — fear and wonder — is the right response to one who commands creation with a word.
26: shadow They mounted up to heaven; they went down to the depths; their courage melted away in their evil plight; Courage melting in the storm — the disciples' terror: they were filled with great fear and said to one another, Who then is this? (Mark 4:41). The storm that tests courage is the occasion for Christ's identity to be revealed. Human courage that melts gives way to the peace Christ bestows: Peace I leave with you; my peace I give to you (John 14:27).
27: shadow they reeled and staggered like drunken men and were at their wits' end. At their wits' end — the desperate condition that drives people to Christ. Peter begins to sink and cries: Lord, save me! (Matthew 14:30). The cry of a man out of all human options is the cry Christ answers immediately — Jesus immediately reached out his hand and took hold of him.
28: type Then they cried to the LORD in their trouble, and he delivered them from their distress. The fourth rescue refrain — enacted in the storm on Galilee. Each refrain of Ps 107 is embodied in the Gospels: wanderers found (v7), prisoners released (v14), sick healed (v20), sailors rescued (v28-30). Christ is the LORD of Ps 107 performing all four deliverances in person.
29: direct He made the storm be still, and the waves of the sea were hushed. The stilled storm — Mark 4:39: the storm becomes still at Christ's word. This is the direct enactment of Psalms 107:29 in his ministry: the LORD who stills storms in the psalm stills them in person through the human Jesus. The hushed waves testify that this man is God.
30: shadow Then they were glad that the waters were quiet, and he brought them to their desired haven. Brought to the desired haven — the rest Christ provides. Come to me, all who labor and are heavy laden, and I will give you rest (Matthew 11:28). The desired haven is ultimately the Father's house: I go to prepare a place for you (John 14:2). Christ pilots every storm-battered soul to the haven of eternal rest.
31: theme Let them thank the LORD for his steadfast love, for his wondrous works to the children of man! The fourth and final refrain — the crescendo of thanksgiving. Revelation 7:9-12: the great multitude from every nation stands before the Lamb and cries: Salvation belongs to our God who sits on the throne, and to the Lamb! — the eschatological fulfillment of the Ps 107 refrain as all the redeemed thank the LORD for his steadfast love and wondrous works in Christ.
32: shadow Let them extol him in the congregation of the people, and praise him in the assembly of the elders. Praise in the congregation — Hebrews 2:12 applies Psalms 22:22 to Christ: in the midst of the congregation I will sing your praise — Christ himself praising the Father in the gathered church. He is the lead worshiper, the firstborn among many brothers (Romans 8:29), praising the Father in the assembly of his people.
33: shadow He turns rivers into a desert, springs of water into thirsty ground, Rivers turned to desert — the judgment side of God's sovereignty. Christ pronounces judgment over unrepentant cities (Matthew 11:20-24): greater works were done there than in Tyre and Sidon, but they did not repent. The rivers of blessing become the desert of judgment when the one who came to save is rejected.
34: shadow a fruitful land into a salty waste, because of the evil of its inhabitants. Fruitful land made barren — the cursing of the fig tree (Mark 11:13-14) enacts this: the fruitless tree that should bear becomes permanently fruitless. The fruitful land made salt is the Israel that rejected its Messiah; the judgment of 70 AD (Matthew 24:2) is the historical embodiment of this principle.
35: shadow He turns a desert into pools of water, a parched land into springs of water. Desert turned into pools — the new creation. Revelation 21:6: to the thirsty I will give from the spring of the water of life without payment. Isaiah 35:6-7 (streams in the desert) is cited in connection with Christ's healings (Matthew 11:5). The desert turned to pools is the eschatological restoration Christ inaugurates.
36: shadow And there he lets the hungry dwell, and they establish a city to live in; The hungry established in a city — the Beatitudes' hungry and thirsty gathered into the kingdom (Matthew 5:3-6). The city they establish is the New Jerusalem: I saw the holy city, new Jerusalem, coming down out of heaven from God (Revelation 21:2). The once-wandering hungry now dwell in the eternal city.
37: shadow they sow fields and plant vineyards and get a fruitful yield. Sowing, planting, fruitful yield — the kingdom parables. The sower (Matthew 13:3-9), the vineyard (John 15:1-8), the harvest (John 4:35-38). Christ plants his disciples in him as branches to bear much fruit. The fruitful yield of v37 is the harvest of the new creation — fruit that abides forever (John 15:16).
38: shadow By his blessing they multiply greatly, and he does not let their livestock diminish. Multiplication by divine blessing — the growth of the church. Acts 6:7: the number of the disciples multiplied greatly in Jerusalem. The fruitful multiplication of v38 is the church's growth through the word of Christ, multiplying in every nation under heaven.
39: shadow When they are diminished and brought low through oppression, evil, and sorrow, Diminished through oppression — the suffering of the church participating in Christ's pattern. We are afflicted in every way, but not crushed... struck down, but not destroyed (2 Corinthians 4:8-9). The diminishment of v39 leads to the reversal of v41 — the pattern of cross and resurrection that defines Christian existence.
40: shadow he pours contempt on princes and makes them wander in trackless wastes; Contempt on princes — the reversal of the powerful. Mary's Magnificat: he has brought down the mighty from their thrones and exalted those of humble estate (Luke 1:52). The cross is the supreme enactment: the princes of this age condemned the Lord of glory (1 Corinthians 2:8), but God exalted the crucified one above every name (Philippians 2:9-11).
41: shadow but he raises up the needy out of affliction and makes their families like flocks. The needy raised from affliction — blessed are the poor in spirit, for theirs is the kingdom of heaven (Matthew 5:3). Christ's resurrection is the paradigm: the one crucified in weakness is raised in power (2 Corinthians 13:4). Those in him share the same trajectory: through affliction into glory (Romans 8:17-18).
42: shadow The upright see it and are glad, and all wickedness shuts its mouth. Wickedness shutting its mouth — Romans 3:19: every mouth may be stopped and the whole world may be held accountable to God. At the final judgment every mouth is stopped before the righteousness of God revealed in Christ (Romans 3:21-26). The upright who see the vindication of Christ are glad with permanent joy.
43: revelation of God Whoever is wise, let him attend to these things; let them consider the steadfast love of the LORD. Wisdom attending to God's steadfast love — Christ is the wisdom of God (1 Corinthians 1:24); considering his steadfast love is the highest wisdom. In him are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge (Colossians 2:3). The wise person of Ps 107's close is the one who sees in Christ the full disclosure of the steadfast love that endures forever.
